question: Explain the difference between a Stack and a Queue. Include a real-world example for each to illustrate their fundamental operational principle.
expectedPoints: A Stack is a Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) structure where the last element added is the first one to be removed.
A Queue is a First-In, First-Out (FIFO) structure where the first element added is the first one to be removed.

A real-world example of a stack is a stack of plates; you add and remove from the top. An example of a queue is a line of people waiting for a service; the first person in line is the first to be served.

question: Compare and contrast the time complexity of the insert and search operations for a Balanced Binary Search Tree (e.g., an AVL tree) and a Hash Table. Discuss one advantage and one disadvantage of each data structure.
expectedPoints: For a Balanced BST, both insert and search operations have an average and worst-case time complexity of O(log n).
For a Hash Table, insert and search operations have an average time complexity of O(1), but a worst-case of O(n) if there are many collisions.

An advantage of a BST is that it maintains sorted order, allowing for efficient in-order traversals. A disadvantage is its higher overhead for balancing. An advantage of a Hash Table is its extremely fast average-case access. A disadvantage is that it offers no inherent ordering of elements.

question: Describe the process of performing a rotation to rebalance an AVL tree after an insertion that causes a violation of the AVL property. Walk through a specific example where a Left-Right rotation (or a Right-Left rotation) is required, detailing each step from the initial insertion to the final balanced tree.
expectedPoints: Identify the first node that became unbalanced (the first ancestor of the inserted node with a balance factor of ±2).
Determine the rotation type based on the insertion path. For a Left-Right case: a left child of the unbalanced node has a right child where the insertion occurred.
Perform the rotation: first, a left rotation on the unbalanced node's left child to turn it into a Left-Left case, then a right rotation on the originally unbalanced node to restore balance.
